food_name: Sundal (South Indian Spiced Boiled Legume Snack)

सुंदल (दक्षिण भारतीय मसालेदार उबले चने का नाश्ता)

chinese: 南印度香料豆 (Nán Yìndù xiāngliào dòu)
malay: Snek Kekacang Rebus Berempah India Selatan
hindi: सुंदल (दक्षिण भारतीय मसालेदार उबले चने का नाश्ता)

region_of_origin: Tamil Nadu, South India (widely consumed in Singapore as a temple prasadam, Navaratri festival food, and Indian hawker snack)
food_subgroup: South Indian dry spiced boiled legume snack — boiled whole legume (chickpeas most common, also black-eyed peas, green gram, cowpeas) tempered with mustard seeds, curry leaves, dried red chilli, and fresh grated coconut

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for sundal specifically. Closest parent concept: cooked legume dish (boiled whole legume with spice tempering). FoodOn does not currently enumerate South Indian tempered legume snacks.
ontology.foodex2_description: South Indian spiced boiled chickpea/legume snack with coconut tempering — closest parent match under FoodEx2 legume dish category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0SAM used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a tempered boiled legume snack; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for sundal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for sundal or South Indian tempered legume snack as of 2026.

preparation_method: dry tossing with spices and fresh coconut
main_ingredients: chickpeas (or black-eyed peas / cowpeas / green gram)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of sundal is approximately 150g, equivalent to a small bowl or a generous temple prasadam portion of spiced boiled chickpeas with coconut tempering.

nutrient_source.title: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 (boiled chickpea / kala chana / kabuli chana entries) with adjustment for coconut and oil tempering
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service FoodData Central SR Legacy + ICMR-NIN 2017
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy boiled chickpea (FDC ID 173756) and ICMR-NIN 2017 boiled chickpea entries. Values adjusted upward for fat (coconut + oil tempering adds ~2–3g fat per 100g) and sodium (~133mg/100g reflecting light salting). Coconut contribution to saturated fat confirmed via USDA SR Legacy coconut (raw, FDC ID 169120). Per-100g values represent the composite dish as prepared.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — chickpeas (garbanzo beans, boiled); University of Sydney GI Database entry for chickpeas mean GI 28 (range 10–40 across varieties and preparations)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 28 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled chickpeas (mean GI 28, range 10–40). Sundal is prepared from boiled chickpeas with dry spice tempering and coconut; the cooking method and legume base are directly analogous. GL per serving calculated as: GI 28 × total carbohydrate per serving (16.0g/100g × 1.5) / 100 = 28 × 24.0 / 100 = 6.72, consistent with input record GL of 5.6 derived using available carbohydrate basis; both confirm very low GL category.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (~200mg per 150g serving) with high potassium (~375mg per serving) yielding a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Sundal is suitable for hypertensive individuals; its low sodium content (~133mg/100g) and high potassium from chickpeas (~250mg/100g) support blood pressure management through a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio, and the absence of added sauces or processed ingredients keeps sodium well within recommended snack limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 28) and very low glycemic load per serving (GL ~5.6) with high dietary fibre (4g/100g) and protein (6.7g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sundal is one of the most appropriate snack choices for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the low GI of boiled chickpeas (GI 28), combined with 4g dietary fibre and 6.7g protein per 100g, produces a sustained and blunted postprandial glucose response, and the coconut fat further slows gastric emptying, making this a clinically favourable snack for glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High soluble fibre (prebiotic galacto-oligosaccharides and inulin from chickpeas), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and moderate saturated fat from coconut (2.5g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sundal is cardioprotective as a snack choice; chickpea soluble fibre supports LDL cholesterol reduction, the dish contains zero trans fat and zero cholesterol, and while coconut contributes medium-chain saturated fatty acids (2.5g/100g), the overall fat profile and high fibre content are consistent with a heart-healthy dietary pattern when consumed in standard serving sizes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High prebiotic fibre supporting gut microbiome health linked to RA inflammatory modulation; high folate (32mcg/100g) and non-haem iron (1.9mg/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sundal is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; chickpea prebiotic fibre (inulin and galacto-oligosaccharides) supports Bifidobacterium-selective gut microbiome modulation associated with reduced systemic inflammation in RA, while folate (32mcg/100g) addresses RA-associated anaemia risk and non-haem iron absorption is enhanced when consumed with vitamin C sources such as lemon juice or green chilli.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sundal is a traditional South Indian dry spiced boiled legume snack, most commonly prepared with chickpeas (kabuli chana) or black-eyed peas, though green gram, cowpeas, and other legumes are also used depending on the occasion. The dish is prepared by soaking and boiling the legumes until tender, then tempering (tadka) in coconut oil or vegetable oil with mustard seeds, dried red chilli, curry leaves, and asafoetida, before tossing with fresh grated coconut and salt. The result is a dry, lightly spiced, nutty-flavoured snack with a firm yet yielding texture from the whole boiled legumes and a fragrant coconut-and-curry-leaf aroma. Sundal holds deep cultural and religious significance in South Indian Tamil tradition: it is the quintessential Navaratri festival food, distributed as prasadam (sacred offering) at South Indian temples on all nine days of the festival, with each day featuring a different legume. It is also a beloved Chennai beach snack sold by vendors at Marina Beach and Elliot's Beach. In Singapore, sundal is available at South Indian temples as prasadam, at Indian hawker stalls, and during Navaratri celebrations. Nutritionally, a 150g serving provides approximately 200 kcal, 10g protein, 8g fat (primarily from coconut and oil), 24g carbohydrate, and 6g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is very low (GI ~28 for boiled chickpeas, University of Sydney GI Database), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 5.6 per serving — making sundal one of the most glycaemically favourable snack foods in the South Indian diet. It is NOVA Class 1 (minimally processed), vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. The high prebiotic fibre content (chickpea inulin and galacto-oligosaccharides) supports gut microbiome health. Sundal is clinically appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (boiled chickpeas, FDC ID 173756) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN 2017 Indian Food Composition Tables, both approved primary sources. GI value of 28 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled chickpeas. Adaptation for coconut and oil tempering is documented and defensible. All mandatory multilingual names present for a Singaporean-context South Indian dish.
notes: water_g set to -1: detailed moisture content for the composite sundal dish as prepared (boiled chickpeas with coconut tempering) is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 173756) or ICMR-NIN 2017 for the finished dish; moisture varies with boiling time and coconut addition, and no approved source reports a single reliable water_g value for the composite preparation. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero: sundal contains no hydrogenated fats; coconut oil and vegetable oil used in tempering contain negligible trans fats confirmed per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero: sundal is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero: no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present; naturally zero for this plant-based legume snack. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero: sundal is vegan with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.2g/100g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.8g/100g) estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profile for boiled chickpeas plus coconut oil contribution; these are estimates and should be treated as approximate. GI source note: University of Sydney GI Database reports mean GI 28 for boiled chickpeas (range 10–40 across varieties); sundal preparation is directly analogous. GL per serving calculated using total carbohydrate method: 16.0g/100g × 1.5 (150g serving) × 28/100 = 6.72, consistent with input record GL of 5.6 using available carbohydrate basis; both confirm very low GL. ICMR-NIN 2017 source URL: https://www.nin.res.in/. FoodEx2 code A0SAM used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for sundal. Aliases consistency check: all strings in aliases_by_language (english, chinese, malay, tamil, hindi, romanized, kannada, telugu) verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array.
